*Alternatively, using the product rule for cross products:*
The derivative of a cross product of two vector functions **a**(t) and **b**(t) is given by:
$$ \frac{d}{dt} [ \mathbf{\text{a}} ( t ) \times \mathbf{\text{b}} ( t ) ] = \mathbf{\text{a}^{\prime}} ( t ) \times \mathbf{\text{b}} ( t ) + \mathbf{\text{a}} ( t ) \times \mathbf{\text{b}^{\prime}} ( t ) $$

$$ \mathbf{\text{u}} ( t ) \times \mathbf{\text{u}^{\prime}} ( t ) = - ( \mathbf{\text{u}^{\prime}} ( t ) \times \mathbf{\text{u}} ( t ) ) $$

Question: The speed of a particle with a position function $$\mathbf{\text{r}} ( t )$$ is $$( \mathbf{\text{r}^{\prime}} ( t ) ) \text{/} ( | \mathbf{\text{r}^{\prime}} ( t ) | ) .$$
o3 Answer:
False.  
The quotient  

  r′(t) / |r′(t)|  

is the unit-length vector that points in the instantaneous direction of motion; it is usually denoted T(t) and called the unit tangent vector.  
Speed, on the other hand, is a scalar quantity—the magnitude of the velocity vector:

  speed  =  |r′(t)|.

Because a vector can never equal a scalar (except in the trivial zero-dimensional sense), the two expressions cannot in general be the same.

Concrete counterexample  
Let  

  r(t) = ⟨t², 0⟩, t ∈ ℝ.

Then  
  r′(t) = ⟨2t, 0⟩,  
  |r′(t)| = |2t| = 2|t|.

Speed: |r′(t)| = 2|t| (a non-negative scalar depending on t).

Claimed expression: r′(t) / |r′(t)| = ⟨2t, 0⟩ / 2|t| = ⟨t/|t|, 0⟩  
which equals ⟨1, 0⟩ if t > 0 and ⟨−1, 0⟩ if t < 0 (and is undefined at t = 0).

The speed 2|t| is not equal to the vector ⟨±1, 0⟩ except when |t| = ½ and we ignore the difference between scalars and vectors, so the statement is false.

In general:  

• r′(t)            → velocity (vector)  
• |r′(t)|          → speed (scalar)  
• r′(t)/|r′(t)| → unit tangent vector (vector of length 1)

Hence the given formula does not represent speed.
